tweets.title: BANGLADESHI SMUGGLER SHOT DEAD BY LOCALS AFTER ILLEGALLY CROSSING INTO INDIA A Bangladeshi national from Sylhet was shot dead on 11 July after crossing around 1 km into Indian territory near the Lakhat area of Meghalaya's East Khasi Hills, according to Bangladeshi officials and local reports. Authorities say he and several others entered India in the early hours of the morning, where members of the local Khasi community opened fire. He was taken back across the border by his companions but later succumbed to his injuries. Initial reports indicate the group may have been involved in illegal cross-border activities, including the collection of betel nuts. The Border Guard Bangladesh (BGB) has raised the matter with the BSF, while both sides have increased vigilance along the border.
tweets.content:
BANGLADESHI SMUGGLER SHOT DEAD BY LOCALS AFTER ILLEGALLY CROSSING INTO INDIA

A Bangladeshi national from Sylhet was shot dead on 11 July after crossing around 1 km into Indian territory near the Lakhat area of Meghalaya's East Khasi Hills, according to Bangladeshi officials and local reports.

Authorities say he and several others entered India in the early hours of the morning, where members of the local Khasi community opened fire. He was taken back across the border by his companions but later succumbed to his injuries.

Initial reports indicate the group may have been involved in illegal cross-border activities, including the collection of betel nuts.

The Border Guard Bangladesh (BGB) has raised the matter with the BSF, while both sides have increased vigilance along the border.

tweets.title: Indian Army to Induct 450 Carl Gustaf M4 Rocket Launchers The Indian Army is set to acquire 450 locally manufactured Carl Gustaf M4 84mm rocket launchers to enhance the firepower of infantry units deployed in Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh. Key features: 🔹 Under 7 kg, significantly lighter than earlier variants 🔹 Effective range of up to 1,000 metres 🔹 Designed to operate in temperatures from +50°C to -20°C 🔹 Compatible with a wide range of indigenously produced ammunition, including anti-tank and high-explosive rounds The Carl Gustaf M4 is being manufactured at Saab's facility in Jhajjar, Haryana, strengthening India's domestic defence manufacturing ecosystem.
tweets.content:
Indian Army to Induct 450 Carl Gustaf M4 Rocket Launchers

The Indian Army is set to acquire 450 locally manufactured Carl Gustaf M4 84mm rocket launchers to enhance the firepower of infantry units deployed in Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh.

Key features:
🔹 Under 7 kg, significantly lighter than earlier variants
🔹 Effective range of up to 1,000 metres
🔹 Designed to operate in temperatures from +50°C to -20°C
🔹 Compatible with a wide range of indigenously produced ammunition, including anti-tank and high-explosive rounds

The Carl Gustaf M4 is being manufactured at Saab's facility in Jhajjar, Haryana, strengthening India's domestic defence manufacturing ecosystem.

tweets.title: I heard someone argue that air-to-air missiles are ordered in tranches because they have a finite service life. That’s correct but that is only part of the story and from very user seller point of view. Take the Indian Air Force’s order for roughly 240 Astra missiles. Yes, a missile has a shelf life, and yes, if it is not kept in a temperature-controlled environment its life can reduce further. All of that is fair. But the real issue is: if you place missile orders with a clear long-term roadmap, the manufacturing ecosystem can plan properly. It knows how much to produce, when to deliver, and where to invest in infrastructure. It can build a little extra capacity to handle crisis situations, so you are not forced to look outside for emergency purchases when demand suddenly spikes. That is why a 20-year roadmap for missiles and manufacturing makes far more sense than simply ordering 240 today and then waiting to see when all the MKI are ready to carry them, or when the Mk1A fleet finally arrives. That should not be just procurement. It should be full lifecycle planning with proper phase out replacement etc built it. Instead we are arguing we will order it when MKI are modified or MK1A are delivered. This very thought process has placed IAF where it stands today.

tweets.content:
BREAKING: IRGC Announces Closure of Strait of Hormuz

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz "until further notice," saying the strategic waterway will remain shut until the United States ends its military intervention in the region, according to Iranian state media.

The IRGC also warned that any military action taken in response to the closure would be met with a "forceful response," adding that additional regional military bases could be targeted if Iran is attacked.

Meanwhile:

🇶🇦 Qatari mediators are reportedly in Iran to help revive U.S.–Iran diplomacy.

🇴🇲 Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met his Omani counterpart in Muscat to discuss regional developments and maritime security.

🇺🇸 President Donald Trump said Washington is willing to continue talks with Iran but maintained that the previous ceasefire is no longer in effect.

The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most critical energy chokepoints, and any sustained disruption could have major implications for global oil markets and regional security.

Ministry of Culture (@MinOfCultureGoI)

One of the most remarkable discoveries from Dholavira, Gujarat, is this monumental Signboard, the largest known inscription from the Indus-Saraswati Civilisation and 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝’𝐬 𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐭-𝐤𝐧𝐨𝐰𝐧 𝐒𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐛𝐨𝐚𝐫𝐝. Comprising 10 large signs/letters made of Gypsum (each sign being appx. 36 cm tall) and the entire inscription stretching nearly 3 metres in length.

The site, located on the arid island of Khadir in Gujarat, was extensively excavated by the @ASIGoI over 13 field seasons from 1989 to 2005, unveiling indigenous urban sophistication in ancient Bharat.

From advanced water engineering to public inscriptions, Dholavira continues to reveal the remarkable capabilities of the Indus-Saraswati people, a legacy deeply rooted in the cultural and civilisational heritage of Bharat.

tweets.content:
BREAKING: India to Open Missile Manufacturing to Private Sector

In a major defence manufacturing reform, the Government of India is set to allow private companies to manufacture missiles, with the DRDO-developed Astra Mk-2 expected to be the first programme opened to industry.

According to reports:
- The Ministry of Defence is expected to invite bids from Indian private firms.
- The Astra Mk-2 is a beyond-visual-range (BVR) air-to-air missile with a reported range of 180–200 km.
- The move aims to expand production capacity to meet the requirements of the Indian Armed Forces and growing export demand.
- Companies expected to participate include Adani Defence, Bharat Forge, Tata Group, Mahindra Group, and ICOMM.

The Pralay tactical ballistic missile may be the next missile programme opened to private industry.
